items.passage:
Resonance is a fundamental phenomenon in acoustics, occurring when an object vibrates at the same frequency as an external driving force. Every physical object possesses a natural frequency at which it vibrates most readily, a property known as its resonant frequency. When the object is driven by an external force whose frequency closely matches this natural value, the resulting vibration grows considerably larger than the small vibrations produced at any other driving frequency.

This dramatic amplification occurs because energy is transferred most efficiently when the timing of each external push aligns with the object's own ongoing motion. A familiar example involves pushing a child on a playground swing: gentle pushes timed to match the swing's natural rhythm produce far greater motion than the same force applied at random intervals. In engineering settings, the same underlying principle allows even a modest sound source to set a thin metal plate humming loudly, provided that the source's frequency falls near one of the plate's resonant modes.

Beyond such simple demonstrations, resonance carries critical implications for the design of tall buildings, long suspension bridges, and acoustic musical instruments alike. Engineers may carefully calculate the resonant frequencies of major structures so that ordinary wind gusts, marching crowds, or passing trains cannot accidentally drive them into destructive oscillation. Musicians often rely on the same principle in reverse, shaping the wooden bodies of stringed instruments so that desired musical frequencies are richly amplified while unwanted overtones gradually fade.
paragraphs: Resonance is a fundamental phenomenon in acoustics, occurring when an object vibrates at the same frequency as an external driving force. Every physical object possesses a natural frequency at which it vibrates most readily, a property known as its resonant frequency. When the object is driven by an external force whose frequency closely matches this natural value, the resulting vibration grows considerably larger than the small vibrations produced at any other driving frequency.

This dramatic amplification occurs because energy is transferred most efficiently when the timing of each external push aligns with the object's own ongoing motion. A familiar example involves pushing a child on a playground swing: gentle pushes timed to match the swing's natural rhythm produce far greater motion than the same force applied at random intervals. In engineering settings, the same underlying principle allows even a modest sound source to set a thin metal plate humming loudly, provided that the source's frequency falls near one of the plate's resonant modes.

Beyond such simple demonstrations, resonance carries critical implications for the design of tall buildings, long suspension bridges, and acoustic musical instruments alike. Engineers may carefully calculate the resonant frequencies of major structures so that ordinary wind gusts, marching crowds, or passing trains cannot accidentally drive them into destructive oscillation. Musicians often rely on the same principle in reverse, shaping the wooden bodies of stringed instruments so that desired musical frequencies are richly amplified while unwanted overtones gradually fade.

questions.stem: The word 'considerably' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: substantially
options.B: gradually
options.C: regularly
options.D: temporarily

questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the 'considerably larger' resonant vibration with the 'small vibrations' produced at other frequencies, indicating a substantial difference in magnitude.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why does resonance produce stronger vibrations than other driving frequencies?

options.A: The driving force is much greater than the object's weight.
options.B: The driving force completely matches the object's outer shape.
options.C: The driving force's timing aligns with the object's own motion.
options.D: The driving force gradually replaces the object's lost energy.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that 'energy is transferred most efficiently when the timing of each external push aligns with the object's own ongoing motion.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about long suspension bridges?

options.A: They are usually built to vibrate at higher frequencies.
options.B: They always collapse when ordinary wind passes through them.
options.C: They have the same resonant modes as musical instruments.
options.D: They may suffer harm when forces match their resonant frequency.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says engineers calculate resonant frequencies so that wind, crowds, or trains 'cannot accidentally drive them into destructive oscillation,' implying that, untreated, such matching can cause damage.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It clarifies the underlying mechanism described as resonance.
options.B: It questions the existence of true resonant frequencies.
options.C: It contradicts the examples given in paragraph 1.
options.D: It presents an alternative theory to the one introduced.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 defines resonance; paragraph 2 then explains the mechanism ('energy is transferred most efficiently when the timing...') and gives the swing example.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention the child on a playground swing in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that resonance is too dangerous in everyday life.
options.B: To illustrate how matched timing produces strong amplification.
options.C: To compare playgrounds with the design of bridges.
options.D: To question whether resonance applies outside of music.

questions.explanation: The swing example is given to make the principle of timing-driven amplification intuitive — 'gentle pushes timed to match the swing's natural rhythm produce far greater motion.'

items.passage:
An alloy is a metallic substance composed of two or more elements, typically combining a base metal with one or more additional metals or nonmetals. Pure metals are often too soft, too heavy, or too reactive for many practical applications, so engineers have long mixed them to obtain desirable mechanical and chemical properties. Bronze, an alloy of copper and tin, is widely regarded as the earliest engineered alloy of significant cultural importance.

This compositional flexibility allows alloys to display behaviors that neither parent metal exhibits alone. When two metals are melted together, their atoms tend to occupy positions within a single crystal structure, distorting the regular lattice in ways that impede the movement of dislocations. This impeded movement translates directly into greater hardness and tensile strength. However, the resulting microstructure may also reduce ductility, and excessive alloying can render a material brittle and unsuitable for tools that must absorb sudden impacts.

Beyond such broad principles, individual alloy systems achieve their performance through highly specific compositional ratios. Stainless steel, for instance, requires at least eleven percent chromium to form a thin, self-healing oxide layer that protects underlying iron from corrosion. Nickel-based superalloys, used in jet engine turbine blades, must retain mechanical strength at temperatures exceeding 1,000 degrees Celsius. Even minor adjustments in trace elements can shift a superalloy from acceptable to catastrophic while in service inside a high-pressure combustion chamber.

Despite decades of empirical refinement, modern alloy research is increasingly conducted in front of computer screens rather than open furnaces. Aerospace firms invest heavily in computational modeling to predict how proposed combinations will behave before any sample is cast, and a single percentage-point change in cobalt content may shift a turbine blade's predicted service life by tens of thousands of hours.

Even minor adjustments in trace elements can shift a superalloy from acceptable to catastrophic while in service inside a high-pressure combustion chamber.

Despite decades of empirical refinement, modern alloy research is increasingly conducted in front of computer screens rather than open furnaces. Aerospace firms invest heavily in computational modeling to predict how proposed combinations will behave before any sample is cast, and a single percentage-point change in cobalt content may shift a turbine blade's predicted service life by tens of thousands of hours.

questions.stem: The word 'impede' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: hinder
options.B: encourage
options.C: balance
options.D: simplify

questions.explanation: The passage uses 'impede the movement of dislocations' alongside 'distorting the regular lattice,' showing that the lattice distortion blocks or hinders dislocation movement.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what is required for stainless steel to resist corrosion?

options.A: A large additional dose of pure nickel within the iron base
options.B: A protective external coating applied during manufacturing steps
options.C: A blend with copper that slows the rate of oxidation
options.D: A minimum content of about eleven percent chromium

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says stainless steel 'requires at least eleven percent chromium to form a thin, self-healing oxide layer that protects underlying iron from corrosion.'

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It contradicts the claim that alloys can become brittle.
options.B: It introduces a different theory of crystal structures.
options.C: It supplies concrete examples of the general principles given.
options.D: It returns to historical alloys mentioned in paragraph 1.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes the general mechanism by which alloys gain strength; paragraph 3 then gives specific systems — stainless steel and nickel superalloys — as concrete illustrations.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 4 about modern alloy research?

options.A: It relies mainly on trial-and-error experiments in open furnaces.
options.B: It increasingly depends on simulations before any sample is cast.
options.C: It has largely abandoned applications in jet engine design.
options.D: It yields only minor improvements over older alloy materials.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 states that modern alloy work is 'increasingly conducted in front of computer screens' and that firms use 'computational modeling to predict how proposed combinations will behave before any sample is cast.'

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Bronze is considered one of the earliest engineered alloys.
options.B: Alloying can sometimes make a material more brittle than expected.
options.C: Pure metals usually outperform alloys in most practical applications.
options.D: Trace elements can strongly affect a superalloy's overall performance.

questions.explanation: The passage states that pure metals are 'often too soft, too heavy, or too reactive for many practical applications,' which directly contradicts the claim that they usually outperform alloys.

items.passage:
During the early period of trans-Pacific contact, sailors and merchants from very different cultures struggled to communicate when their ships first met in port. Their spoken languages shared almost no vocabulary, and complex contracts often had to be negotiated through awkward gestures and rough sketches drawn on deck. As trade volumes grew rapidly during the eighteenth century, these communication barriers caused frequent misunderstandings, lost cargo, expensive legal disputes, and even violent confrontations that endangered the entire trading network.

This serious problem was eventually addressed through the gradual development of trade pidgins, simplified languages that borrowed words from each participating community. Pidgins typically used a small number of basic terms, a fixed word order, and clear hand signals to support meaning. Traders on both sides could learn enough of a pidgin in only a few weeks, and many port cities maintained simple schools or apprenticeships where young clerks were systematically trained in the local trading speech. Over time, these schools became important centers of cultural exchange in their own right.

These pidgins greatly reduced the frictions of early trans-Pacific exchange and allowed long-running partnerships to form between communities that once viewed each other with deep suspicion. However, pidgins remained limited tools for ordinary commerce and rarely supported nuanced discussions of religion, philosophy, or detailed scientific ideas. Some linguistic researchers suggest that the very simplicity of a pidgin may have discouraged broader cultural exchange, leaving deeper understanding to later generations who painstakingly learned each other's full languages.

However, pidgins remained limited tools for ordinary commerce and rarely supported nuanced discussions of religion, philosophy, or detailed scientific ideas. Some linguistic researchers suggest that the very simplicity of a pidgin may have discouraged broader cultural exchange, leaving deeper understanding to later generations who painstakingly learned each other's full languages.

questions.stem: The word 'frictions' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: harsh weather patterns
options.B: physical damages
options.C: practical difficulties
options.D: religious disagreements

questions.explanation: In context, 'frictions of early trans-Pacific exchange' refers to the misunderstandings and disputes described in paragraph 1, that is, the practical difficulties of doing business.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what helped traders learn a pidgin within only a few weeks?

options.A: A network of professional translators in port cities
options.B: A series of written contracts in many languages
options.C: A small vocabulary combined with a fixed word order
options.D: A required immersion period of several years abroad

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that pidgins 'typically used a small number of basic terms, a fixed word order, and clear hand signals to support meaning,' allowing rapid learning.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It rejects the effectiveness of pidgins as trade languages.
options.B: It supplies a historical example of pidgins in Europe.
options.C: It compares pidgins with modern translation technology.
options.D: It evaluates both the benefits and the limits of the solution.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 introduces pidgins as the solution; paragraph 3 then weighs their successes against their limits in religion, philosophy, and science.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention 'awkward gestures and rough sketches drawn on deck' in paragraph 1?

options.A: To argue that nonverbal signs were the only effective method.
options.B: To illustrate how limited communication was before pidgins emerged.
options.C: To compare port communication with later written commercial contracts.
options.D: To suggest that drawing skills were a key trade asset.

questions.explanation: The reference is used to illustrate the inadequacy of early communication, setting up the introduction of pidgins as the solution in paragraph 2.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true about pidgins EXCEPT

options.A: Pidgins were rich enough to support detailed religious discussions.
options.B: Pidgins helped reduce violent confrontations between trading partners.
options.C: Pidgins relied on a small set of borrowed vocabulary words.
options.D: Pidgins were taught to young clerks within port-city schools.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 explicitly states that pidgins 'rarely supported nuanced discussions of religion, philosophy, or detailed scientific ideas,' contradicting option A.

items.passage:
For centuries, naturalists treated the stone-like remains buried in cliffs and quarries as curiosities rather than as biological evidence. Early collectors generally assumed that such forms had grown spontaneously within the rock itself, perhaps through some mineral process that mimicked the shape of living organisms. Without systematic excavation or comparative anatomy, the deeper meaning of these objects remained almost entirely beyond reach, and any biological interpretation that did appear was typically dismissed as speculation by established scholars.

This early uncertainty began to give way during the eighteenth century, as a small number of European naturalists started documenting the precise rock layers in which particular forms were found. Although their efforts revealed striking patterns, including extinct species not visible among living animals, these patterns were often interpreted within rigid theological frameworks that resisted any notion of deep geological time. While the data accumulated steadily, interpretive disputes over its meaning persisted for decades and slowed broader scientific acceptance.

A genuine breakthrough arrived only with the development of stratigraphic correlation and modern paleontology during the nineteenth century. Researchers known as biostratigraphers showed that distinctive fossil assemblages could be used to identify rocks of the same age across separate continents. This correlation in turn provided a reliable framework for reconstructing the history of life on Earth, and it offered a powerful new tool for the still-young petroleum industry that depended on locating ancient sedimentary basins.

While the data accumulated steadily, interpretive disputes over its meaning persisted for decades and slowed broader scientific acceptance.

Researchers known as biostratigraphers showed that distinctive fossil assemblages could be used to identify rocks of the same age across separate continents. This correlation in turn provided a reliable framework for reconstructing the history of life on Earth, and it offered a powerful new tool for the still-young petroleum industry that depended on locating ancient sedimentary basins.

questions.stem: The word 'speculation' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: practical experiment
options.B: detailed analysis
options.C: minor revision
options.D: untested guesswork

questions.explanation: The passage contrasts biological interpretations with the lack of 'systematic excavation or comparative anatomy,' so 'speculation' here implies unsupported, untested guessing.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what limited the acceptance of new fossil interpretations during the eighteenth century?

options.A: A lack of stratified rock outcrops in Europe
options.B: A tendency to interpret findings through theological frameworks
options.C: A shortage of skilled excavators across major sites
options.D: A growing rivalry between competing paleontologists

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 notes that 'patterns were often interpreted within rigid theological frameworks that resisted any notion of deep geological time.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about the petroleum industry?

options.A: It benefited from advances in fossil identification techniques.
options.B: It funded most of the early biostratigraphic research efforts.
options.C: It required only relative age estimates, never absolute ones.
options.D: It collapsed when reliable stratigraphic tools became available.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says biostratigraphic correlation 'offered a powerful new tool for the still-young petroleum industry,' implying that the industry profited from the new identification methods.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It contradicts the claim that early collectors lacked any data.
options.B: It contrasts continental and oceanic fossil records of the era.
options.C: It traces a transitional stage between early ideas and modern ones.
options.D: It explains religious doctrines that supported the early collectors.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 describes the earliest, pre-scientific stage; paragraph 2 then describes the eighteenth-century middle stage in which data accumulated but interpretation was still contested.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention 'extinct species not visible among living animals' in paragraph 2?

options.A: To show that the rock record contained extinct life forms.
options.B: To argue that paleontologists could not identify any living species.
options.C: To compare extinct species directly with rare modern animals.
options.D: To question whether such species had ever truly existed.

questions.explanation: The phrase introduces a striking pattern from the rock record, illustrating that fossil evidence pointed to species no longer present among living animals.

items.passage:
Cooperative breeding is a reproductive strategy in which individuals other than the parents help to raise the offspring within a tight social group. This behavior, observed in roughly nine percent of bird species and in many social mammals, may include feeding young chicks, defending the nest from predators, or even teaching foraging skills. The non-parental contributors, often called helpers, are typically older offspring from previous breeding cycles who have not yet established territories of their own.

This unusual arrangement is generally explained by the principle of kin selection. Because helpers share a substantial portion of their genes with the offspring they assist, supporting the family group can indirectly propagate their own genetic material. Helpers also tend to gain practical experience that improves their own chances of successful reproduction later. However, the system can break down when local conditions change abruptly, since unrelated immigrants entering the group may receive no genetic benefit from continued assistance.

Beyond its biological interest, cooperative breeding offers insight into the evolution of complex social structures. Studies of cooperative birds have suggested that the long-term survival of small populations may depend heavily on the presence of helpers, since their assistance buffers nesting failure during particularly harsh seasons. Conservation programs in some regions therefore monitor helper numbers as carefully as they monitor breeding pairs, and any sudden drop is treated as an early warning that the broader population may soon decline.

Because helpers share a substantial portion of their genes with the offspring they assist, supporting the family group can indirectly propagate their own genetic material. Helpers also tend to gain practical experience that improves their own chances of successful reproduction later. However, the system can break down when local conditions change abruptly, since unrelated immigrants entering the group may receive no genetic benefit from continued assistance.

Conservation programs in some regions therefore monitor helper numbers as carefully as they monitor breeding pairs, and any sudden drop is treated as an early warning that the broader population may soon decline.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The kin selection theory that guides modern conservation programs.
options.B: The decline of helper birds in many modern animal populations.
options.C: The structure and significance of cooperative breeding in animals.
options.D: The role of teaching in mammal social group survival.

questions.explanation: The passage defines cooperative breeding, explains its kin-selection mechanism, and discusses its broader significance — together describing the structure and importance of the strategy.

questions.stem: The word 'propagate' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: replace
options.B: spread
options.C: weaken
options.D: hide

questions.explanation: In the context of passing on genetic material indirectly through helping kin, 'propagate' means to spread the helper's genes through related offspring.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, who typically acts as a helper in cooperative breeding?

options.A: Older offspring without territories of their own
options.B: Unrelated immigrants from neighboring social groups
options.C: Adult parents from earlier failed breeding attempts
options.D: Younger siblings still under their parents' direct care

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 explicitly states helpers 'are typically older offspring from previous breeding cycles who have not yet established territories of their own.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about unrelated immigrants?

options.A: They are usually welcomed because they bring new genetic material.
options.B: They become the most frequent helpers in large social groups.
options.C: They typically replace the parents within a single breeding season.
options.D: They have little kin-selection incentive to remain as helpers.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 notes that 'unrelated immigrants entering the group may receive no genetic benefit from continued assistance,' implying weak kin-selection motivation.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It rejects the kin selection principle as an explanation.
options.B: It extends the topic to applied conservation contexts.
options.C: It returns to the historical origins of the strategy.
options.D: It contrasts mammalian and avian cooperative systems.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains why cooperative breeding evolves; paragraph 3 then extends the discussion to the practical role of helpers in conservation.
